item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image comes from here and is copyrighted, and the second image cannot be found to have a cc-compliant source. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems kinda bland to me. If more the article was focused on this supposed dimension or at least their attempts get information about it, it could have been really interesting. Also, if you're going introduce sentience/sapience to and object it seems lazy to do so in the last few lines of the article and even more lazy to hide what could potentially be the most interesting bit of information behind a [DATA EXPUNGED]. This could really use a total revamp.

item.title: Terminalogical issues.
item.content:encoded:
This SCP item in it's current state is well written, but unfortunately there is one problem which is the terminology.
Delta radiation is already a specific term used to refer to a specific type of radiation:
"A delta ray is a secondary electron with enough energy to escape a significant distance away from the primary radiation beam and produce further ionization"[1]:25, and is sometimes used to describe any recoil particle caused by secondary ionization." https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delta_ray

I would suggest an edit in which a note is added for clarification stating that 'delta radiation' refers to the particle rather than the overall type of radiation.
In this case the type of particle that corresponds to the chosen name for the anomalous radiation would be the Delta Baryon. https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delta_baryon Seriously look up (Delta particle) and (Delta radiation) and you will find the Wikipedia pages I have posted links to.

One can always simply claim that the fungus itself containing highly anomalous biological mechanisms produces or relays a stabilizing force which allows free Delta baryons to last longer before breakdown and thus display previously unseen properties and that said force seeps through from the fungus's home world at the site of the mineral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is just sorta boring, I dunno, can't really give any more specific criticism. Nothing about the object is inherently interesting, and the attempt to make it so by suggesting sapience just feels tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Expunging the "language" feels really cheap. Liked the rest enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its cool i guess. unless im missing something its just some sort of fungi hive-mind from another dimension that wants to drag other dimension back home to help grow? this only just classifies as Keter thanks to the near impossibility of containment and lack of a current cure, feels like it could do with something like; "when attempting to destroy infestation [DATA EXPUNGED], breach events are to be contained with all available force and MTF-'Magic Shrooms' has been formed to facilitate this'. just my two-sense and i dunno if something should go in the data expunged bit but im not creative enough to think of something. it just doesn't feel dangerous enough to warrant Keter outside of difficulty in containment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was confused at the first few sentences of the description, but then I read a little more and got hooked.
Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude! Is it just me or is it the cause of freakin' glowing mushroom biomes in Terraria!… I'm sorry, this is probably really childish compared to the whole discussion… (it explains the whole "different universe" thing… not really)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Magnetic shielding can stop the alpha and beta particles, sure but it is kind of over overkill for that when you can stop those things with just walls.

The reason why you'd want to use it on a spacecraft is that you can conceivably block stuff without any secondary radiation products in your shielding material and therefore get away with only a thin aluminum hull cladding.

That's not really an issue in a room.

The "delta" radiation here appears to be more like EM radiation than ionizing radiation. Magnets probably aren't very effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would this be able to shield SCP-059's radiation? https://www.sciencenews.org/article/magnetic-bubbles-could-shield-astronauts-radiation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having read this over, I think it has a solid foundation but lacks any kind of a stinger to take what you just learned about this SCP and make you feel one way or another about it.

It's crazy. It's right there at the cusp. I don't find that it could be considered bad ('transdimensional sapient fungus-creating element' is certainly not something someone could say is "overdone") but in it's current iteration, but it does seem underwhelming.

It isn't quite "woah" or "meh" but rather "huh."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP sounds better when someone reads it to you in Russian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only problem with calling it 'delta radiation' is that it kind of implies it's ionizing (which I don't think was meant by the author) and that it would be able to penetrate practically any form of physical matter (which I have no problems with, considering the isolation and shielding specified in the containment procedures).

Still, it's much better than it was when I last read it, a pretty long time ago. I'm not downvoting, but I don't think it's interesting enough to award an upvote. It just works too much like an SCP-020 that spreads through radiation instead of spores, specially considering its behavior-changing properties in humans.

Maybe it would be more interesting if it focused more on the mineral aspects (i.e. giving affected people [whose behaviour would be practically unchanged] an almost invisible 'shielding' of radioactive crystal shards embeded in their skins that would eventually burst and 'infect' other inorganic materials, turning them into other instances of the SCP), all in all, I'm not a big fan of the fungi thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. This is only a skip because it feeds on radioactivity from some other reality, whereas we have our own, home-grown Chernobyl-shrooms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Came across the original source of the second pic on tumblr. This is an art project titled Seizure by Roger Hiorns. Basically he filled the room up with copper sulphate solution and let it chill for a few days, and drained the room. This is the result.

Video!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's… okay. I'm kinda neutral on it; despite the rather in-your-face assertion that it's from another dimension, there isn't much that's interesting about it. Also, calling a radiation that causes nothing more than radiation-immune fungi doesn't really necesitate calling it "delta radiation." I'm sure others will disagree with me, but it just irks me to call it "delta radiation," or really any Greek letter-radiation. It feels out of place.

If this had an interesting experimental log or something to go along with it like, say, SCP-051 did, then I'd consider an upvote. Until then, I'm not swayed.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Putting this article up for review. Please take the time to re-read and potentially re-rate it.

Personally, I had not read the rewrite before, and I enjoyed it. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I finally read the rewrite. Not bad.

There's still quite a lot of downvotes from its old days, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I just like this SCP as a mineralogical anomaly. Maybe that would make it too bland, but that's just me. The interdimensional fuzzy mushrooms just don't do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Delta radiation" gives me hives. I know it's a holdover from the original, but it still bothers me a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually kind of like it - its weird and dangerous enough to be an SCP, but I feel like it is a nice change from all the world-ending/population-destroying SCPs that are so prevalent on this site (nothing wrong with the latter either, but its nice to see a weird SCP that doesn't cause the world to end). The only problem I have is that its classified as Keter - its mostly contained, therefore I'd think that it should be re-classified as Euclid. Other than that, nice article, +1.

Cancel most of that. I am a biologist at heart, and I just re-read it and found horrific gaps in the biology. I didn't downvote, but I canceled my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Everything's blue in this world, the deepest shade of mushroom blue." This is a portal into Trent Reznor's brain, isn't it?

+1. As I already like it, I'm not sure what to suggest to improve it for people who don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there anything I can do to get folks to actually like this one?
It's gone from -12 to -2, but I'm not satisfied with "hate it less than the old one."
Radioactive blue fungus needs love too, you know…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I was actually thinking re: the radiation: The fungus is radioactive. It's still radioactive when it's dead, so killing it in an infected person (aside from the damage to converted tissue) removes the immunity, but it's still in your system irradiating you.

Also, the "immunity" may not consist of preventing damage from radiation, but just keeping the victim alive long enough to be converted. Radiation poisoning can take a while to kill someone; look up the "walking ghost" phase.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you're saying here now.

A lethal dose of radiation could still take a month to kill someone, particularly if there was something radioactive lodged in the person's body making sure all the vital organs and bone marrow was being dosed.

It could be considered that allowed to grow, the fungus would be replacing vital tissues that were damaged, but removed "early" it left the person with radiation damage that would be hard to recover from.

There's a thin line between "sloppy wording" and "OMFG too many words" in what should be looked at as an executive summary. The wording may look a little loose, but I'm honestly hard pressed as to how to phrase things better without it becoming a word salad. (For said samples of word salad, take a look at my posting history.)

Maybe just inserting "essentially" right before the first immune would be enough to imply without going into long details, while cleaning up the line about losing immunity would tighten things up without having to go into details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That reasoning is already present in the article, and it is the problem with "late-stage treatment". The problem is that "early stage treatment" is likely to cause death by radiation poisoning. I'd be happy with this article if the danger in early-stage treatment was that the small amount of converted tissue is in a critical position (lung tissue, heart muscle, etc) instead of radiation poisoning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The myth is that the bacteria in their mouth is what makes their bite so lethal. I think it was back in 2004 that someone located the venom in their saliva which led to an updated view of their lethality. There is still a (metric)crap ton of bacteria in their mouths however.

There's been some theorizing that the bacteria still plays a role in the lethality of their bite due to a the victim's immune system being compromised by the venom, but I'm not current on the research so I have no idea if that's still a current theory being explored or not.

Since they are endangered and the most likely way of one to die from "natural causes" involves being eaten by another dragon, I imagine getting specimens to dissect is rather difficult.

-Edit:
Went ahead and read up on Wikipedia. The venom was theorized in 2005, with a preserved venom gland extracted in 2008. It's important to note that dragons in zoos have much cleaner lifestyles and do not have the same bacterial infestation as their wild brethren. As such, there's been some bites that people survived from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually komodo dragons don't have special bacteria, that is a myth. They have pouches of poison in their jaws like a gila monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a valid point. Here is how I'm interpreting it, and it relies on a bit of context that I think isn't in the article anymore. Eric_h might come in and correct me on his intent.

The fungus is (or was at least) radiotrophic and comes from a place that's inundated with radiation. Part of its effects is being mildly radioactive itself while consuming radiation.

When infecting a biological organism it's consuming radiation which makes the host "resistant" to ionizing radiation because the fungus is absorbing the bulk of it, while it is also replacing the host's tissues with itself. The low levels of radiation would be harmful, but since the fungus is replacing the host's tissues it doesn't really matter. They are being held together by the fungus.

If the fungus is removed after it's progressed after a certain point, you're pretty much left with just a mess.

So, in a nutshell, it's not video game immunity, but more an immunity in effect. Aka how a Komodo dragon is "immune" to infections from the bacterial cultures thriving in its mouth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig the rewrite. Downvote changed to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But we're actually being exposed to low levels of radiation at all times on Earth; if this thing makes you more vulnerable to radiation, perhaps it would make even harmless ambient radiation dangerous to those infected by it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing that's bugging me about this article: "Treated individuals lose their immunity to radiation, and will already have absorbed a now lethal dose prior to treatment."

To use an analogy, imagine a fungus that conveys immunity to fire, and you get it by walking into a specific fire. Once you walk out of the fire, getting an antifungal treatment won't cause death by burning, because you're not standing in a fire anymore. Radiation poisoning is like getting burned. If you're somehow immune to the damage while you're exposed to the radiation, removing the immunity won't hurt you as long as you're not still being irradiated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Google images…

The SCP-059 rock is actually a piece of scheelite (tungsten ore) under UV light.

The infested room is a piece of a strange art project where the walls of a room are covered with copper sulphate crystals: http://www.flickr.com/photos/mykreeve/2991924267/ I did a little photoshop on it to blur the crystals, and make the shade of blue a bit more intense and unnatural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this revision. The pictures in particular are cool; where'd you find them?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding Quik's vote against deletion of the current page, for identical reasons, and also because -10 (which is where it stands now) is perfectly recoverable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rewrite posted. We'll see what happens.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Gah, the packrat in me is saying no, don't delete it 'cos we'll lose the page history and discussion thread, and if it can be rescued, all the better… but I can also get behind the re-writers wanting a fresh start.

Voting against deletion. The archivist in me wins this round. But, if the existing article is updated but still has a negative rating, I'd certainly be open to deletion/posting fresh (I realize that there are generally different reactions to updated articles vs. new articles).

item.title: Re: A possibility for revision
item.content:encoded:
I guess it could have gone this way, but I kind of like what I ended up doing, where it's more of an invasion tool or terraformer from some alternate reality.
Or maybe it's the way alien radioactive fungus tries to have a friendly chat with people.

item.title: A possibility for revision
item.content:encoded:
Is it possible that the spheroid shape of SCP-059 is because it was intended as the core of an implosion-type fission bomb? Any irregularities may be due to weathering/melting/etc prior to discovery.

It certainly would make an excellent weapon - a one-two punch of nuclear and biological attacks. And this would be a realistic explanation for its shape, which has been mentioned as a sticking point.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Third.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: -11 is pretty recoverable but seconding for the purpose of reposting a fresh take.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: We have a rewrite, but the author wants a fresh page. Well, why the hell not. Re-re-beginning deletion vote at -11.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if someone on Staff is up for burning the old one, I'll post my version and we'll see how it goes. I don't really want it starting at -11, that's kind of a big handicap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect with the hour of posting and people's attention being drawn to the more active pieces (zomg more AWCY? articles), people probably missed your post.

I'm going to reread it later to see if anything pops out, but I liked the re-write.

I think it fixes most of the common complaints posted in 2011. Also, the pictures you used are far more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tweaked the rewrite a bit more, but if no one really likes this thing, I'm not going to put it up.

(Are you folks just not reading the rewrite, or is this The Giant "Meh" of Silence?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of a form of light and/or radiation which causes an unknown species of fungus to grow on anything hit by it, but really that is the only saving grace of this SCP or any alterations of it. Personally, I think we should take this off the site as soon as possible and keep the lightfungus idea for another SCP.

Sorry but I really don't like this SCP very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here is a first try at rewriting this thing:

http://scpsandboxwiki.wikidot.com/eric-h-s-sandbox

Feedback welcome, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My feeling is for this to work, the fungus has to do something more interesting than infect and kill people. That should be the real hook of this thing, if it's going to work the way Hurtz suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: interdimensional jock itch?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno man, I kinda like what hurtz came up with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That…sounds fucking awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That actually makes sense. I didn't like the fungus since it seemed like a totally random effect, but I can see it working with that explanation behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've probably read around 10% of the SCP's on the site. I can immediately think of 2 SCP's that involve a substance transforming things into things like itself, with maybe a third. I can think of 1 for sure that gets more intelligent as people are converted, and I dimly remember at least 2 others or at least I think I do. Personally I'm not a fan of rehashing any of those approaches.

There are a couple of little things that could be cleaned up in the current article. The extreme temperature and containment procedures should probably be reconciled. Downgrading it to Euclid since it's apparently not explosive and the fungal infection isn't communicable. Possibly describing it as something other than a spheroid to avoid the knee-jerk crowd. If you want to keep the picture, just don't call it a meteorite since no meteorite looks like that. (I'm not quite sure what it actually is a picture of though.) Unfortunately, none of those are likely to save the article.

Unlike some of the other posters, I actually like the extra-dimensional fungal infection aspect.

This is how I picture it all working:

SCP-059 is partially extra-dimensional and the stress on reality is what is causing the high levels of radiation as it's acting as a catalyst for other things to bleed through. The "delta" radiation particularly doesn't strictly radiate solely in our universe.

Instead, it acts like a flashlight in a dark room, illuminating parts of our universe to another reality that is apparently vastly overrun with fungus. This illumination is what allows the fungus to "follow" it back to our universe. The fungus is noted as radiotrophic, which would make sense if its home reality was overran with gamma radiation (which is also flowing back into our reality.). It would grow slowly if not exposed to radiation, while it would grow quickly if it was near SCP-059 or a nuclear reactor.

Really my only thought at the moment is to go even farther with the extra-dimensional fungus. Only growing on humans is just wrong. It should grow on any exposed organic matter. Likewise, it should probably cross over onto non-organic matter as well at a much slower rate, relying on the radiation pouring out of SCP-059 to keep alive.

Containment would then have to have 3 elements; Handling the extreme heat and gamma radiation, which the current article doesn't do all that well on; A shield for the "delta radiation", which the existing article uses the water tank for; A constant effort for sanitizing the fungus which should be trying to cross over, which isn't addressed at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm…maybe it becomes sentient as it converts people, and has a language based on emitting different types/levels/frequencies of radiation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeh….space rocks that cause random space-time anomalies seems a little bit played out. I don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been kicking around some ideas for how this could work better, but am not sure I like what I have. I'll tell you what I've been thinking, and you can tell me if I should write it up in SCP form:

OK. SCP-059. Extradimensional thing emitting extradimensional radiation. I don't like that it is a random sphere. I don't like the fungus effect of the radiation. Let's say SCP-059 started out as a meteorite. Maybe a

+ Glowing Blue Rock
- OK, seen it

and the main effect is the radiation makes SCP-059 contagious, in the sense that (almost) anything exposed to the radiation for more than a certain amount of time turns into more SCP-059 material. i.e. a few weeks after the glowing blue rock fell, you had this:

+ It's spreading!
- OK, seen it

Containment? Maybe some super-dense material is not affected and blocks the radiation. Something hard to get a large amount of, like iridium. So the Foundation can contain a small piece of this stuff, but has to figure out how to destroy large amounts of it found when it has spread.

A twist: Maybe the extradimensionality has another side effect — it can temporarily open up a portal to a random place within X km, and irradiate things at a distance. So the Foundation has to worry about chasing down additional "infestations" of this stuff, and is worried that there is some of it underground or undersea, turning the Earth into SCP-059. Also, this means it's a difficult item to experiment on safely. If it's out of containment too long, it's spreading… If we want to keep this Keter, it's a good reason for it to be Keter.

Think I should go with this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So….is the rewrite happening? Or is this doomed?

item.title: Re: Seems a lot of people like this one, so...
item.content:encoded: Probably not, but it's been around for a really long time. I remember reading this when I first came around.

item.title: Re: Seems a lot of people like this one, so...
item.content:encoded: is this actually used in any tales?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's back up to -3 but I suspect that it won't last too long there.
